title: Arrays & HashMap — ROI & Pattern
oneSentence: Trade O(N) space for O(1) lookups — use a HashMap to avoid nested loops.
description: Master the HashMap lookup pattern twice. First through guided line-by-line tracing of Two Sum, then by reconstructing the core steps.

roi.complexity: Time: O(N) | Space: O(N) — one-pass with HashMap
roi.signals: Unsorted array pair finding, complement lookup, frequency counting, grouping by property.

codeLines.text: public static int[] solve(int[] nums, int target) {
// STEP 1 — INIT: Create the guest book (HashMap) to remember who's arrived
Map<Integer, Integer> seen = new HashMap<>(); // KEY = number, VALUE = index
// STEP 2 — LOOP: Walk through each person at the party
// STEP 3 — CHECK: "What partner do I NEED? Is that partner already in the guest book?"
return new int[] { seen.get(need), i }; // partner found → return both indices
// STEP 4 — STORE: "No partner yet → sign the guest book so someone can find me later"
seen.put(nums[i], i); // store AFTER checking → prevents self-matching

traceSteps.title: 1. INIT — Create the Guest Book
traceSteps.text: Create an empty HashMap called 'seen'. The KEY stores the number we've visited, and the VALUE stores its index. This is our 'guest book' — O(1) lookup to check if a complement has already arrived.

traceSteps.title: 2. LOOP — Walk Through Each Element
traceSteps.text: Iterate through the array with index i from 0 to nums.length - 1. Each element is a 'person at the party' — we check if their complement is already in the guest book before signing in.

traceSteps.title: 3. CHECK — Compute Complement & Lookup
traceSteps.text: Calculate 'need = target - nums[i]' — this is the complement. Then check if 'need' already exists in the HashMap. If it does, we found our pair: return the stored index and current index i. The HashMap gives us O(1) lookup instead of a nested O(N) scan.

traceSteps.title: 4. STORE — Sign the Guest Book
traceSteps.text: If no complement was found, store the current number and its index in the HashMap: seen.put(nums[i], i). We store AFTER checking to prevent a number from matching with itself (e.g., target=6, nums[i]=3 would falsely match).

traceSteps.title: 5. RETURN — No Pair Found
traceSteps.text: If the loop completes without finding a valid pair, return an empty array. This handles the edge case where no two numbers in the array sum to the target.

sandbox.question: What data structure gives O(1) lookups for complements?
Why must you CHECK the map BEFORE storing the current number?
options: To avoid matching a number with itself

sandbox.question: What is the complement formula for Two Sum?

followUps.q: What if the data is too large to fit in memory (streaming data)?
followUps.a: For exact lookups, you might need distributed caching (Redis) or database indexes. For probabilistic existence checks, use a Bloom Filter.

followUps.q: How does Java's HashMap handle collisions?
followUps.a: It uses chaining (LinkedLists). If a bucket gets more than 8 items, Java automatically converts the LinkedList to a Red-Black Tree to degrade gracefully from O(1) to O(log N) instead of O(N).

followUps.q: If data is constantly changing, how to keep the map updated efficiently?
followUps.a: Use a `ConcurrentHashMap` in multithreaded environments to prevent locking the entire map during updates.

followUps.q: Can you solve this with O(1) space instead of using a HashMap?
followUps.a: Usually requires sorting the input first (which takes O(N log N) time), then using Two Pointers. You trade O(N) space for O(N log N) time.

followUps.q: What happens when the load factor is exceeded?
followUps.a: The HashMap undergoes 'rehashing'. It creates a new array double the size and redistributes all existing entries. This is an O(N) operation but amortizes to O(1) over time.
